coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanofluids to improve oil recovery from carbonate and sandstone oil reservoirs
coredata.dc:creator: Shayan Nasr, Mahdi
Esmaeilnezhad, Ehsan
Allahbakhsh, Ahmad
Choi, Hyoung Jin
coredata.dc:description: Nanofluids based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are an emerging nanotechnology with potential applications in enhanced oil recovery (EOR). Herein, we report the EOR performances of N-GQDs that have different GQD-sizes and nanofluid concentrations. Wettability and hydrocarbons/water interfacial tension (IFT) studies establish that N-GQDs nanofluids at concentrations of 0.5 mg/mL and with the lowest mean GQDs size range (about 3.5 nm) lead to more wettability alteration of carbonate rocks and decrease the oil/water IFT and n-heptane/water IFT from 16.88 mN/m to 0.975 mN/m, and from 50.24 mN/m to 27.87 mN/m, respectively. Moreover, core flooding experiments show that the aforementioned nanofluid provided greater than 18% and 14% enhancements in the oil recovery factors from carbonate and sandstone core samples, respectively, versus water flooding. In the carbonate core, the alterations of wettability and IFT reduction simultaneously promote oil recovery factors. By comparison, for the case of sandstone core, only the reduction in oil/water IFT leads to higher oil recovery factors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enhanced oil recovery
Graphene quantum dots
Wettability alteration
Interfacial tension reduction
Core flooding

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: NPs and nanofluid synthesis; PEI-derived N-GQDs
Preparation and characterization of cores and slabs
Contact angle, IFT, and viscosity measurements

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: One of the authors (HJC) acknowledges a particle support from National Research Foundation of Korea ( 2018R1A4A1025169 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of contact angle measurement (a) and IFT measurement setup (b).

Schematic illustration of core flooding setup; (1) hydraulic oil, (2) pump, (3) accumulator, (4) water or brine, (5) nanofluid, (6) oil, (7) bypass valve, (8) distributor, (9) core plug, (10) confining pressure, (11) confining sleeve, (12) pressure gauge, (13) outlet.
TEM images of 3-N-GQDs (a) and 12-N-GQDs (b).
DLS results of 3-N-GQDs (a), 6-N-GQDs (b), 9-N-GQDs (c) and 12-N-GQDs (d).
AFM results of 3-N-GQDs (a) and 12-N-GQDs (b).
UV–Vis spectra of synthesized 3-N-GQDs, 6-N-GQDs, 9-N-GQDs and 12-N-GQDs samples.
FT-IR spectra of prepared N-GQDs samples.
Oil contact angles after treatment of carbonate rocks with different nanofluids.
Schematic illustration for justifying the effect of NPs concentration on the intensity of repulsion forces.
Oil contact angles after treatment of carbonate rocks with different concentrations of 3-N-GQDs.
Oil contact angles after treatment of sandstone rocks with different nanofluids.
Oil contact angles after treatment of sandstone rocks with different concentrations of 3-N-GQDs.
IFT alterations between oil/fluids (water and nanofluids).
IFT alterations between n-heptane/fluids (water and nanofluids).
IFT alterations between oil/nanofluids with different concentrations of 3-N-GQDs.
IFT alterations between n-heptane/nanofluids with different concentrations of 3-N-GQDs.
Results of core flooding in carbonate core; pressure drop (open), oil recovery factor (closed).
Results of core flooding in sandstone core; pressure drop (open), oil recovery factor (closed).
Schematic illustration of EOR mechanisms in carbonate core (a) and sandstone core (b).
Properties of sandstone and carbonate cores.
Viscosity of water and different nanofluids at 1000 s−1 at room temperature.
The pH values of each nanofluid at concentration of 0.5 mg/mL.
Comparison between the impacts of the proposed 3-N-GQDs with several other NPs in the best state (after two PV injection of nanofluids) on the oil recovery factor.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanofluids to improve oil recovery from carbonate and sandstone oil reservoirs

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Inha University, Incheon 22212, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofluids based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are an emerging nanotechnology with potential applications in enhanced oil recovery (EOR). Herein, we report the EOR performances of N-GQDs that have different GQD-sizes and nanofluid concentrations. Wettability and hydrocarbons/water interfacial tension (IFT) studies establish that N-GQDs nanofluids at concentrations of 0.5 mg/mL and with the lowest mean GQDs size range (about 3.5 nm) lead to more wettability alteration of carbonate rocks and decrease the oil/water IFT and n-heptane/water IFT from 16.88 mN/m to 0.975 mN/m, and from 50.24 mN/m to 27.87 mN/m, respectively. Moreover, core flooding experiments show that the aforementioned nanofluid provided greater than 18% and 14% enhancements in the oil recovery factors from carbonate and sandstone core samples, respectively, versus water flooding. In the carbonate core, the alterations of wettability and IFT reduction simultaneously promote oil recovery factors. By comparison, for the case of sandstone core, only the reduction in oil/water IFT leads to higher oil recovery factors.

ce:section.ce:para: For decades, technological developments have yielded tremendous demand for energy that caused an exponential growth in global oil consumption [1]. Yet, given the decreased exploration of new oil reservoirs, one method to meet new energy demand could be to increase oil production from mature oil reservoirs that are currently in service but nonetheless operating inefficiently. Typically, only about one-third of the initial oil in a reservoir can be harvested by natural production processes, while the remainder remains intact in the reservoir. In these cases, enhanced oil recovery (EOR) methods, which are techniques for extracting oil from reservoirs from which oil cannot be extracted based on its original pressure, can help improve the oil yield [2].

EOR techniques are classified into several categories including chemical, thermal, and pressure maintenance methods, of which chemical methods are very popular. The main chemical EOR methods are based on polymers and surfactants. For polymer-based EOR methods, the shear viscosity of the injection fluid is controlled by polymers to modify the mobility ratio [3]. By comparison, EOR techniques based on surfactants exploit surfactants to control the interfacial tension (IFT) between the production and injection fluids. This modifies the wettability of porous media such that it tends to be wetted by the injection fluid, thus the oil recovery can be increased. While chemical EOR methods are promising for improving crude oil extraction, these methods can have negative environmental consequences, such as impacting underground water, that serve as obstacles for their wide application. Therefore, alternative EOR techniques, including nanofluid systems, have attracted great attention in recent years.

Nanofluid-based EOR techniques employ nanofluids to improve crude oil extraction by means that are similar to those for chemical EOR methods. Specifically, EOR systems that use nanofluids can promote alterations in wettability [4], reduce IFT [5], and modify the mobility ratio [6]. Moreover, nanofluids can reduce the viscosity of extra-heavy oil [7] or serve as an added agent to facilitate conventional polymer/surfactant flooding methods [8]. The high EOR performances of nanofluids originate from the size of the nanoparticles (NPs) that comprise nanofluids. The sizes of the pores in porous media are in the range of micrometers and, as a result, the relatively smaller NPs can easily enter and move within these pores [9,10]. Silica NPs have been used in most EOR experiments [11], but a few studies investigated the effects of the other types of NPs (such as carbon-based NPs) for EOR.

Several research groups have reported that carbon-based NPs can improve the oil recovery factor from porous media. Soleimani et al. [12] observed an about 20% enhancement in the oil recovery factor by using a nanofluid system with carbon nanotubes, mainly because the carbon nanotubes altered wettability and reduced IFT. Moreover, Jafarbeigi et al. [13] reported that surface-modified graphene nanosheets can increase the oil recovery factor from carbonate cores by as much as 23% versus water flooding as secondary flooding. These results demonstrate that nanofluids with carbon nano-allotropes can be highly effective for EOR.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a family of carbon-based NPs that have a lateral size range of under 20 nm [14]. Over this range, the structural characteristics of graphene nanosheets are completely different due to the prevalence of quantum confinement effects. Such variations in structural characteristics lead to extraordinary properties that make these NPs strong candidates for many applications, including drug delivery, biosensors, and nanofluids [15]. Key characteristics of GQDs include high water solubility [16,17], low toxicity [16,17], high specific surface areas [17,18] and a large number of available sites for functionalization [17,19]. Moreover, these NPs can be easily doped with a variety of dopants, making them tailorable for EOR applications.

This work explores how the lateral size of nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) in nanofluids influence their performance in EOR applications. Till now, most of the NPs studied for nanofluid-based EOR applications are silicon-based, while the study of other potentially useful NP classes, such as carbon nano-allotropes have been neglected. The impact of nanofluid concentration on the EOR performance is also studied here in detail. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first systematic study of the potential of N-GQDs-based nanofluids for EOR applications. Recently, however, AfzaliTabar et al. [20] reported on the use of a nanofluid based on the nanohybrid N-GQDs/MoS2 for EOR, specifically demonstrating that this hybrid nanomaterial system can improve oil recovery factors from carbonate cores by up to 20% versus water flooding. Thus, insights from our study provide a new understanding about the potential of GQDs-based nanofluids for EOR applications in carbonate and sandstone porous media. Here, we gain this insight by systematically interrogating the effects of GQDs-based nanofluids with respect to wettability, IFT between oil/water and between n-heptane/water, viscosity, and EOR from carbonate and sandstone core plugs.

Branched polyethylenimine (PEI) (weight-average molecular weight by light scattering: 800 g/mol, catalog No. 408719) and NaCl were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. and Merck Co, Germany, respectively, and were used without any purification. The n-heptane was also supplied from Merck Co, Germany. Synthetic brine used for water flooding was prepared by dissolving NaCl at 42000 ppm in distilled water (DW). Carbonate and sandstone rocks were obtained from outcrops of two reservoirs in southwest Iran for contact angle and core flooding experiments. The properties of these rocks are listed in Table 1 . The crude oil had an API and density of 32.292 degrees and 0.8639 g/cm3, respectively, at 60 °F and supplied from a reservoir in southwest Iran.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NPs and nanofluid synthesis; PEI-derived N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization process [14,21]. In a typical synthesis, a required amount of PEI was dissolved in the deionized water by using magnetic stirring. 20 mL of the solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (30 mL total volume) and hydrothermally treated for 3, 6, 9, or 12 h in an electronic oven at 190 °C to prepare the 3-, 6-, 9-, and 12-N-GQDs samples, respectively. The color of the solution changed from colorless to pale orange during the hydrothermal carbonization process, which indicated the formation of N-GQDs. A shift from a pale orange to orange color was observed by extending the duration of the hydrothermal process. Synthesized NPs were dissolved in DW (0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg/mL) and these prepared nanofluids were used for all experiments without further treatment.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy measurements were performed on a Shimadzu FTIR-8400S spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were conducted on a 150 kV Philips CM30 (3-N-GQDs and 12-N-GQDs) microscope and a 100 kV Philips CM20 (6-N-GQDs and 9-N-GQDs) microscope. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) tests were carried out on a Dualscope DS 95–200, DME AFM and used samples on freshly cleaved mica. The AFM was equipped with a rectangular aluminum-coated cantilever used a scan rate of 5 μm/s in non-contact mode. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) studies were conducted at 25 °C on a Nanotrac Wave II Particle Size Zeta Potential Analyzer. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were acquired by using a NanoDrop 2000 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The pH values of each nanofluid were determined using a pH meter (Metrohm AG-827 pH lab, Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of cores and slabs
ce:section.ce:para: A core plugging system (Fars EOR Co., Iran) was used to create core plugs that were used in core flooding experiments. Initially, a cutter (Fars EOR Co., Iran) was employed to trim two ends of the sandstone and carbonate cores. For contact angle measurements, common 1-in. diameter cores from rocks were cut into several pieces that had a 0.5 cm thickness and subsequently polished by using a common procedure that is described in the literature [22]. An unsteady state gas permeation apparatus (Petro Pazhouhesh Ahoura Co., Iran) was used to measure the porosities and gas permeabilities of the core plugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contact angle, IFT, and viscosity measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared slabs were initially aged in oil at 60 °C and 1 atm for 24 h and subsequently dried in an oven for 6 h at 60 °C [23]. Afterward, the oil-infused slabs were submerged in nanofluids and DW for 72 h. The oil contact angle, which is the angle between the submerged slab and aqueous fluid, was recorded by a camera and calculated by ImageJ software. A schematic illustration of the wettability measurement system is shown in Fig. 1(a). When the oil contact angle is below 90o, nearly equal to 90o, or greater than 90o, the wettability of the corresponding system is defined as oil-wet, intermediate wet, and water wet, respectively [24]. Experiments have proven that changes in the wettability of porous media from oil-wet to water-wet are associated with greater factors of oil recovery [25–27].

The IFT between oil/water-nanofluids and n-heptane/water-nanofluids were measured by using an IFT-400 tensiometer (Fars EOR Co, Iran) at four different nanofluid concentrations, with either 0 (i.e, neat DW), 0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg/mL of NPs. IFT measurements were based on the pendant drop method, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Initially, the quartz chamber was filled with water or each nanofluid and a needle was used to create an oil droplet. Images captured from this system were subsequently analyzed to calculate IFT values. The pendant drop method uses two experimental parameters to calculate the oil/water IFT, which are the equatorial diameter (de) and the diameter (ds) at a distance de from the top of the oil drop. In this method, the IFT is calculated by using the following Eq. [28],
(1)
IFT =
∆ ρg
d e 2
H
where g is gravitational acceleration, Δρ is the density difference between the water/nanofluids and the oil/n-heptane drop, and H is the shape factor that can be obtained from the table provided by Bartell and Niederhauser, [29] as a function of the ds/de ratio.
Furthermore, the shear viscosity of the each nanofluid was measured by a rotational viscometer (RST-CC, Brookfield, USA) at 1000 s−1 at room temperature.

A systematic procedure was followed for core flooding measurements. First, core plugs were washed with DW to remove all impurities. Then, the core plugs were dried in an electronic oven at 60 °C for 1 h. Synthetic brine was then injected to the cores in two steps, from each of the two ends of the cores at an injection rate of 0.5 cc/min, up to the full saturation (forced imbibition) to enable the calculation of liquid permeability. Cores were then aged in synthetic brine for 30 days (spontaneous imbibition) and then oil was injected at an injection rate of 0.2 cc/min until no water was produced. The cores were then aged in oil for 14 days and then used in measurements to simulate EOR. The cores were injected with synthetic brine, the selected nanofluid, and another round of synthetic brine to perform the water flooding, nanofluid flooding, and post-flush water flooding steps, respectively. All of the flooding operations were conducted by using a common setup [30–32] that is shown in Fig. 2 . The values of oil produced and differential pressure (∆P) were recorded at various times during the experiment. The following Eq. [33] was used to calculate the oil recovery factor,
(2)
Cumulative Oil Recovery
%
=
Produced Oil
cc
Oil in place
cc
× 100

TEM and DLS results were used to investigate the influences of the duration of the hydrothermal synthesis process on the lateral size of synthesized N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. 4 , respectively. 3-N-GQDs had a mean diameter of 3.5 nm and a size range of 1.5–9.5 nm, as evidenced by TEM results. Extension of the hydrothermal carbonization process from 3 to 12 h notably increased the lateral size of synthesized N-GQDs, as the lateral size of 12-N-GQDs was in the range of 160–180 nm. However, larger particles can also be observed in TEM results of 12-N-GQDs (Fig. 3 (b)). Moreover, the lateral size range of N-GQDs prepared through 6 and 9 h of the hydrothermal synthesis process also shown a similar increasing trend suggesting the size of synthesized N-GQDs increased with the duration of the synthesis process (see Fig. S1 for TEM results of 6-N-GQDs and 9-N-GQDs).

DLS results were used to further investigate the lateral size range of N-GQDs synthesized via different hydrothermal process durations (Fig. 4). The increase of the lateral size range of synthesized N-GQDs with the duration of the hydrothermal process is distinguishable in DLS results in Fig. 4. The average lateral size of 3-N-GQDs was clearly lower than 5 nm. The lateral size range increased to 80–300 nm, 100–800 nm, and 300–1000 nm for 6-N-GQDs, 9-N-GQDs, and 12-N-GQDs samples, respectively.

Investigations by AFM proved the thicknesses of N-GQDs prepared by using 3 h and 12 h hydrothermal carbonization times, as shown in Fig. 5 . The 3-N-GQDs had an average thickness of 0.8 nm. Increasing the duration of the hydrothermal carbonization time from three to 12 h yielded N-GQDs that not only had greater diameters but also thicknesses. Specifically, the thicknesses of 12-N-GQDs were about 2–3 nm (Fig. 5(b)), which is close to the mean thickness of graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets [34,35]. Thus, increasing the duration of the hydrothermal carbonization process altered the size of N-GQDs toward the dimensional characteristics of GO nanosheets [36]. Yet, even after 12 h of hydrothermal carbonization, the resulting 12-N-GQDs still had disk-like forms with lateral dimensions that were much lower than GO nanosheets (Fig. 5(b)).

UV–Vis studies further investigated the role that the duration of the hydrothermal carbonization process had on N-GQD structure, as shown in Fig. 6 . The UV–Vis spectrum of 3-N-GQDs shows two main characteristic absorption bands at 240 and 370 nm. The absorption band at about 240 nm corresponds to the π → π* transitions of sp2 carbon atoms in GQDs [14]. The broad band observed at around 370 nm is assigned to overlapping peaks associated with the n → π* transition of C=O bonds (COOH edge groups) [14] and the transition of conjugated C-N/C=N bonds [37]. Interestingly, the absorption band associated with the C-N/C=N transition underwent a blue-shift to 310 and 300 nm for N-GQDs prepared by hydrothermal carbonization for 6 and 9 h, respectively. These shifts were also accompanied by an increase in the intensity of the C-N/C=N band. Interestingly, this band increased in intensity and underwent a red-shift for N-GQDs prepared by using a 12 h hydrothermal carbonization process. These behaviors indicate that the duration of the hydrothermal carbonization process influences the structures and sizes of N-GQDs.

FT-IR spectra of 3-N-GQDs, 6-N-GQDs, 9-N-GQDs, and 12-N-GQDs samples are presented in Fig. 7 . The FT-IR band observed at 815 cm−1 is assignable to the bending vibrations of C–O bonds, while the bands detected at 1014 and 1117 cm−1 correspond to the α- and β-region stretching vibrations of C–O containing groups, respectively [14,38]. Moreover, bands observed at 1309 and 1461 cm−1 arise from C–O γ-region stretching vibrations [14,39]. The band observed at 1569 cm−1 could reflect overlapping signals from sp2-hybridized C=C in-plane stretching vibrations of backbone carbons [14,40] and the N–O stretching vibrations [41] of nitrogen atoms in the N-GQD structure. The band at 1641 cm−1 can also include overlapping signals from two bonds, in particular the C=O stretching vibrations of edge carboxyl groups and the C=N stretching vibrations of backbone nitrogen atoms [41]. The band observed at 2885 cm−1 is attributed to the N–H and C–H stretching vibrations of carbon and nitrogen atoms. The characteristic band of O–H stretching vibrations was also detected at around 3400 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs. No significant difference was observed in the FT-IR spectra of 3-N-GQDs, 6-N-GQDs, 9-N-GQDs, and 12-N-GQDs, suggesting that variations in the duration of the hydrothermal synthesis process had no effect on the surface functionalities of synthesized N-GQDs. The only obvious difference was in the intensity of the band related to the C=C bonding in the backbone of N-GQDs (1569 cm−1), which increased with an increase in the duration of the hydrothermal synthesis process.

Contact angle measurements were conducted to establish the contact angle of oil in the presence of each nanofluid and, as a comparison, DW. According to Fig. 8 , the contact angle of oil in presence of DW was 22.79 degrees, indicating that carbonate slabs are oil-wet. Contact angle results for N-GQDs of various sizes at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL are provided in Fig. 8. The 3-N-GQDs increased the oil contact angle from 22.79 to 120.85 degrees, which was the greatest effect that any of the GQDs tested here exerted on the wettability of oil-wet carbonate samples. These data demonstrate that the wettability state of carbonate slabs changed to a water-wet state under the effects of 3-N-GQDs. Moreover, larger N-GQDs promoted oil contact angles that were closer to the contact angle of oil/water (oil/without N-GQDs) (or initial oil-wet condition). Indeed, N-GQDs of smaller sizes changed surface properties more effectively because smaller N-GQDs can penetrate the rock surface and more easily integrate between the carbonate rock and oil droplets, leading to greater intensive disjoining pressures [42].

Structural disjoining pressure is a mechanism that has been reported in various articles that describes changes in the wettability of oil-wet rocks in the presence of NPs [43–47]. Specifically, the structural disjoining pressure is the pressure required to overcome the adhesion force between the fluid (oil) and solid surface (slabs), causing the fluid to detach from the surface. According to this mechanism, NPs create a wedge film with the oil on the oil/water/rock interface and, as such, the size and concentration of NPs become key factors in modulating the resulting surface disjoining pressure. As the NP size decreases, the effects of Brownian motion increases such that raising the NPs concentration concomitantly increases the intensity of repulsive forces, amounting to a greater structural disjoining pressure [48].

The Stokes-Einstein equation [49] corroborates the effect that reducing NP particle size increases Brownian motion,
(3)
DB =
K B
T
3 п
μ f
D P
where DB is the Brownian diffusion coefficient (m2/s), KB is the Boltzmann constant (J/K), T is the temperature (K), μf is the viscosity of the fluid (N·s/ m2), and DP is the diameter (m) of the NP.

Coulomb's law illustrates the relationship between NPs concentration and electric force at the wedge film as follows [50],
(4)
F =
K ×
q 1
×
q 2
ε bf
×
r ×
ϕ
− 1 / 3
2

According to this equation, if F > 0 indicates a repulsive force and F < 0 indicates an attractive force. Moreover, K is the Coulomb constant (9 × 109 Nm2/C2), q1 and q2 (C, Coulomb) are respective charges of NP1 and NP2, εbf represents the dielectric constant of the medium, r is the particle radius (m), and ϕ denotes the volume fraction of the NPs. For this study, NP1 and NP2 are of the same type and thus both have the same negative charge, which leads to an F > 0 that establishes repulsive forces among NPs. As schematically illustrated in Fig. 9 , higher NP concentrations in the nanofluid and wedge film increase the volume fraction of NPs (ϕ) and, according to Coulomb's equation, result in stronger repulsive forces among NPs.

Based on the aforementioned theory and experimental contact angle measurements, 3-N-GQDs were the best sample with respect to changing wettability and thus were further explored to investigate the effect of N-GQDs concentration on the final contact angle.

The 3-N-GQDs showed the best performance among the four N-GQDs for modifying the wettability of oil-wet carbonate slabs. As such, additional contact angle tests were conducted for 3-N-GQDs samples at three concentrations (0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg/mL) to evaluate the influence of N-GQDs concentration on the wettability of oil-wet carbonate slabs.

The results (Fig. 10 ) reveal an optimum N-GQDs concentration (0.5 mg/mL) below which the oil contact angle on the oleophilic carbonate slabs decreased, but above which the efficiency of N-GQDs to alter wettability decreased. These results are consistent with the literature [51,52]. As the N-GQDs concentration increases to approach the optimum concentration (0.5 mg/mL), the number of N-GQDs between the carbonate rock and oil droplet increase and promote stronger repulsive forces and structural disjoining pressure. Thus, the oil contact angle increased, but higher N-GQDs concentrations reduced the stability of NPs in the base fluid and wedge film due to the higher charge density in the wedge layer [53]. Consequently, the capacity of NPs to alter the wettability of carbonate slabs was reduced. Moreover, N-GQDs concentrations above a specific point induced agglomeration, and hence the particles could not completely cover the surface of the rock and change its wettability.

In addition, similar experiments were performed for sandstones to evaluate the effect of nanofluids on the oil contact angles, and their results are presented in Fig. 11 and Fig. 12 . It appears that the effect of N-GQDs was negligible due to electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged sandstone slabs and negatively charge N-GQDs prevent the adsorption of particles onto the sandstone surface [54].

Similar to surfactants, NPs accumulate at the oil/water interface to reduce IFT. The reduction in IFT becomes more pronounced for smaller NPs because greater numbers of NPs can accumulate at the interface. Moreover, smaller NPs increase the NP/oil contact area because more molecules of asphaltene can adsorb onto the surface of carbon-based NPs, thereby inhibiting asphaltene molecules from precipitating on the oil/water interface [51,56]. These two mechanisms explain the trend that the smallest N-GQDs (3-N-GQDs) decreased IFT more than the larger N-GQDs. Thus, the 3-N-GQDs appear to be the best in terms of reducing IFT and were advanced to further studies that interrogated the effect of 3-N-GQDs concentration on IFT at concentrations of 0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg/mL.

The wettability and IFT experiments show that 3-N-GQDs are the most effective for modifying the interfacial properties of the oil/water/rock and oil/water interfaces. Moreover, 0.5 mg/mL appeared to be the optimal concentration for 3-N-GQDs.

The alteration of the wettability of porous media, reduction in oil/water IFT, and modification of the viscosity of injected fluids are the specific factors involved in chemical methods for EOR. Surfactants can alter wettability and reduce oil/water IFT, while polymers can modify the viscosity of injected fluids. The novel N-GQDs, especially 3-N-GQDs, demonstrably altered wettability and reduced oil/water IFT and, in this section, their capacity to adjust viscosity is investigated. Table 2 provides the viscosity of water and nanofluids at an N-GQDs concentration of 0.5 mg/mL at 1000 s−1, at which all nanofluids exhibited nearly the same viscosity with respect to water. Therefore, the aforementioned factor of viscosity adjustment does not greatly impact the EOR process.

Previous experiments in this study show that the four synthesized N-GQDs, in particular the 3-N-GQDs nanofluid, effectively alter wettability and reduce IFT but do not significantly influence the viscosity of water as an injection fluid.

Because carbonate formations can be affected by the pH of the injected fluids, the pH of each nanofluid at concentration of 0.5 mg/mL was measured, and the results are presented in Table 3 . As can be deduced, all used nanofluids are not acidic, therefore this parameter will not affect the oil recovery values in carbonate core flooding measurement.

Two pore-volumes (PV) of the top-performing N-GQDs (3-N-GQDs) at their optimal concentration (0.5 mg/mL) were used for carbonate and sandstone core flooding measurements, the results of which are described below. Before and after nanofluid flooding, two steps of flooding were carried out with synthesized brine as water and post flush water flooding, respectively.

The ∆P and recovery factor for flooding measurements using the carbonate core are shown in Fig. 17 . These data show that the breakthrough occurs after about two PV of water flooding, at the point where the ∆P reaches its maximum (1632 psi) after which the ∆P decreases. The 3-N-GQDs nanofluid has a viscosity that is similar to water and thus produces a ∆P that closely aligns with that produced by water. The oil recovery factor after flooding the carbonate core with water is 62%, but nanofluid flooding with two PV yielded a greater oil recovery factor of 80% that after post water flooding became 85.97%. This demonstrates that the 3-N-GQDs nanofluid increases the oil recovery factor by 18% versus water flooding, which originates from both the reduction in IFT and alteration of the wettability of porous media from oil-wet to the water-wet state. After post water flooding the oil recovery factor reached 85.97% as a result of increased alterations in wettability due to a longer interaction time of the nanofluid with the porous media during post water flooding. Similar results were observed by Izadi et al. where, after post water flooding, the recovery factor increased by about 14% and 24% compared to Fe3O4 nanofluid flooding in sand-pack columns and carbonate cores, respectively [59].

For flooding measurements using the sandstone core, (Fig. 18 ) breakthrough occurred after about 1.3 PV of water flooding, at which point the differential pressure ∆P reaches a maximum (1100 psi) and subsequently decreases. As shown in Fig. 18, the oil recovery factor after water flooding is 35%, but after nanofluid flooding the value increases to 49%. Finally, after the post water flooding step the oil recovery factor reached 64.5%. Thus, the nanofluid increases the oil recovery factor by 14% compared to water flooding, which from only the IFT reduction as mentioned elsewhere [60].

These experiments establish that 3-N-GQDs improve oil recovery by 18% and 14% compared to water flooding in carbonate and sandstone cores, respectively. The main mechanisms of EOR by which these NPs operate are altering the wettability and reducing IFT in carbonate core (Fig. 19(a)), while only reducing IFT is important in sandstone core (Fig. 19(b)).

Table 4 compares the results of the oil recovery factor obtained in this study with several other studies, which show that 3-N-GQDs performed much better in terms of EOR than several other nanomaterials in both carbonate and sandstone cores.

Dispersions of the N-GQDs of different sizes synthesized by controlling the duration of the hydrothermal reaction in water were used to flooding sandstone and carbonate cores for applications in EOR. Longer reaction times yielded N-GQDs of larger sizes. Wettability experiments using carbonate rocks established that nanofluids with smaller N-GQDs altered the wettability more favorably, with 3-N-GQDs showing the best results. Separately, concentration-dependent tests showed that 0.5 mg/mL was the optimal concentration of 3-N-GQDs among three different concentrations, while the N-GQDs nanofluids did not change the wettability of sandstones. Moreover, the efficiency of nanofluids to reduce oil/water IFT and n-heptane/water IFT became greater for smaller N-GQDs. The 3-N-GQDs nanofluid at 0.5 mg/mL concentration was also found to be the optimum sample with respect to oil/water IFT and n-heptane/water IFT decreased from 16.88 mN/m to 0.975 mN/m, and from 50.24 mN/m to 27.87 mN/m, respectively.

While the viscosity of all nanofluids is the same as that of water, viscosity is observed to be not an effective parameter to influence the oil recovery factor in core flooding experiments. Finally, the core flooding tests were performed on sandstone and carbonate cores by using the optimum nanofluid system (3-N-GQDs at 0.5 mg/mL). For the carbonate core, the nanofluid increased the oil recovery factor by 18% versus water flooding. Such improvements arise in part from the nanofluid altering the wettability toward the water-wet state and reducing the IFT between oil and water. By comparison, the nanofluid improved the oil recovery factor in the sandstone core by 14%, because of the only oil/water IFT reduction and without the effect of any wettability alteration mechanism in this system. Therefore, our results demonstrate that the N-GQDs nanofluid with the smallest N-GQDs size is effective for EOR from carbonate and sandstone cores.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: One of the authors (HJC) acknowledges a particle support from National Research Foundation of Korea (2018R1A4A1025169).
